startScreenOptions.title: PY6.1-PY6.9 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for PY6.1-PY6.9.

interactions.label: Which alveolar cell type is responsible for secreting surfactant?

params.question: Which alveolar cell type is responsible for secreting surfactant?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Type I pneumocytes are flat cells that cover most of the alveolar surface and facilitate gas diffusion — they do not produce surfactant.
Correct. Type II pneumocytes are cuboidal cells that secrete surfactant, a phospholipid mixture that reduces alveolar surface tension.
Capillary endothelial cells form the innermost layer of the respiratory membrane — they participate in gas exchange, not surfactant production.
Bronchial epithelial cells line the conducting airways and help warm, filter, and humidify air. Surfactant is produced only in the alveoli by Type II pneumocytes.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember the two alveolar cell types: Type I (flat, for diffusion) and Type II (cuboidal, for surfactant secretion). This distinction is clinically important in neonatal RDS.
Type II pneumocytes secrete surfactant (mainly DPPC), which prevents alveolar collapse by reducing surface tension proportionally to alveolar size.

interactions.label: Elastic fibres in the lung parenchyma contribute two-thirds of the lung's elasti

params.question: Elastic fibres in the lung parenchyma contribute two-thirds of the lung's elastic recoil, while alveolar surface tension contributes one-third.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: The ratio is the other way around. Surface tension at the alveolar air-liquid interface provides two-thirds of the lung's elastic recoil, and elastic fibres provide one-third. This explains why surfactant deficiency in premature neonates causes such severe respiratory distress.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement is reversed. Surface tension at the alveolar air-liquid interface provides two-thirds of elastic recoil, while elastic fibres contribute only one-third. This is why surfactant deficiency has such a dramatic effect on lung mechanics.

interactions.label: A patient is breathing rapidly and shallowly at 30 breaths per minute with a tid

params.question: A patient is breathing rapidly and shallowly at 30 breaths per minute with a tidal volume of 200 mL. Despite a minute ventilation of 6 L/min (similar to normal), why does this patient become hypoxaemic?

answers.text: The high breathing rate causes excessive carbon dioxide washout from the alveoli

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Excessive CO₂ washout (hypocapnia) occurs with deep rapid breathing, not shallow breathing. Here, most of each breath never reaches the alveoli.

answers.text: Most of each breath ventilates the anatomical dead space, so very little air reaches the alveoli

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. With 150 mL of dead space per breath, only 50 mL of each 200 mL breath reaches the alveoli — giving an effective alveolar ventilation of just 1.5 L/min instead of the normal 4.2 L/min.

answers.text: Rapid breathing causes bronchospasm, increasing airway resistance

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Tachypnoea itself does not cause bronchospasm. The problem is a dead space fraction issue — most of each shallow breath never participates in gas exchange.

answers.text: The respiratory muscles fatigue, reducing the force of each inspiration

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While muscle fatigue can occur in prolonged tachypnoea, the immediate cause of hypoxaemia is the unfavourable dead space-to-tidal volume ratio, not reduced inspiratory force.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key concept is alveolar ventilation = (tidal volume − dead space) × respiratory rate. With shallow breathing, the dead space fraction becomes so large that effective alveolar ventilation plummets, even if minute ventilation appears normal.
With a tidal volume of 200 mL and dead space of 150 mL, only 50 mL per breath reaches the alveoli. Alveolar ventilation drops to 1.5 L/min — far below the 4.2 L/min needed for adequate gas exchange.

interactions.label: During intense exercise, which combination of local tissue changes shifts the ox

params.question: During intense exercise, which combination of local tissue changes shifts the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve to the RIGHT, facilitating oxygen unloading?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: These conditions shift the curve to the left, causing tighter oxygen binding — the opposite of what exercising tissues need.
Correct. Remember CADET — CO₂, Acid, 2,3-DPG, Exercise, Temperature all shift the curve rightward, reducing haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen so more is released to active tissues.
These conditions all shift the curve to the left. Exercising tissues actually increase 2,3-DPG, lower pH (acidosis), and raise temperature — all rightward shifts.
CO₂ shifts right, but decreased temperature and alkalosis shift left. During exercise, all three factors — CO₂, temperature, and pH — shift in the same rightward direction.

overallFeedback.feedback: Use the CADET mnemonic for right shifts: Carbon dioxide, Acid, 2,3-DPG, Exercise, Temperature. All increase during exercise, collectively reducing haemoglobin's oxygen affinity and promoting unloading at the tissues.

The CADET mnemonic captures all right-shift factors: CO₂ ↑, Acid (H⁺) ↑, 2,3-DPG ↑, Exercise, Temperature ↑. This ensures that active tissues, which produce heat and CO₂, receive more oxygen precisely when they need it.

interactions.label: In the pulmonary circulation, local hypoxia causes vasodilation to increase bloo

params.question: In the pulmonary circulation, local hypoxia causes vasodilation to increase blood flow and improve oxygen delivery to poorly ventilated alveoli.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This is a common mix-up. In systemic circulation, hypoxia causes vasodilation to increase oxygen delivery. But in the pulmonary circulation, hypoxia causes vasoconstriction — blood is diverted away from poorly ventilated alveoli toward better-ventilated ones. This mechanism optimises V/Q matching.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement describes the systemic response, not the pulmonary one. In the lungs, hypoxia causes vasoconstriction, diverting blood away from poorly ventilated alveoli toward well-ventilated regions. This is the opposite of systemic circulation, where hypoxia causes vasodilation.

interactions.label: In a patient with chronic COPD and CO₂ retention, why can administering high-flo

params.question: In a patient with chronic COPD and CO₂ retention, why can administering high-flow oxygen be dangerous?

answers.text: Oxygen directly depresses the medullary respiratory centres

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Oxygen does not directly depress the medullary centres. The problem is that chronic CO₂ retention has reset the central chemoreceptors, making hypoxic drive the primary stimulus for breathing.

answers.text: High oxygen removes the hypoxic drive, which has become the primary stimulus for breathing due to central chemoreceptor reset

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Chronic CO₂ retention causes central chemoreceptors to tolerate high CO₂ levels. The peripheral chemoreceptors' response to low oxygen becomes the main breathing trigger. Removing hypoxia with high-flow O₂ eliminates this drive, causing hypoventilation and CO₂ narcosis.

answers.text: Excess oxygen converts haemoglobin to methaemoglobin, impairing oxygen delivery

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Methaemoglobinaemia is caused by oxidising agents like nitrites and dapsone, not by supplemental oxygen. The danger in COPD relates to loss of the hypoxic ventilatory drive.

answers.text: High-flow oxygen causes bronchoconstriction, worsening airway obstruction

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Supplemental oxygen does not cause bronchoconstriction. The risk is hypoventilation from removing the hypoxic drive that chronic COPD patients depend on after their central chemoreceptors have adapted to high CO₂.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key concept is chemoreceptor adaptation. Central chemoreceptors normally drive 80% of ventilation via CO₂/H⁺ sensing. In chronic COPD, they reset to tolerate high CO₂. Peripheral chemoreceptors responding to hypoxia become the main driver — removing that hypoxia with high-flow O₂ removes the last stimulus to breathe.

In chronic COPD, persistently elevated CO₂ resets the central chemoreceptors. The carotid body response to low PaO₂ becomes the primary ventilatory stimulus. High-flow oxygen removes this trigger, leading to hypoventilation and potentially fatal CO₂ narcosis.
